title: C++ Type Conversion
content:
### Type Conversion in C++

Type conversion (type coercion) happens automatically or explicitly when values of different types are used together.

---

### Implicit Conversion (Automatic)

The compiler automatically converts between compatible types:

```cpp
int a = 10;
double b = a;          // int to double: 10.0
char c = 65;           // int to char: 'A'
bool d = 0;            // int to bool: false
int e = 3.14;          // double to int: 3 (truncation warning)
```

---

### Conversion Hierarchy

```
bool < char < short < int < unsigned < long < long long < float < double
```

---

### Arithmetic Conversions

When mixing types in expressions:

```cpp
int i = 5;
double d = 2.5;
auto result = i + d;   // int promoted to double: 7.5
auto result2 = 5 / 2;   // Integer division: 2
auto result3 = 5 / 2.0; // Double division: 2.5
```

---

### Numeric Promotion & Demotion

| Conversion | Direction | Risk |
|-----------|-----------|------|
| Promotion | Smaller to larger type | Safe |
| Demotion | Larger to smaller type | Data loss possible |

```cpp
int large = 1000;
char small = large;   // Demotion: potential overflow
```

---

### User-Defined Conversion

```cpp
class Fahrenheit {
public:
  Fahrenheit(double temp) : temp_(temp) {}
  operator double() const { return (temp_ - 32) * 5.0 / 9.0; }
private:
  double temp_;
};
```

---

> **Key Point:** Be aware of implicit conversions. When precision matters, use explicit casts to avoid unexpected data loss.
